category_summary:
The **GPT-5.6** [family launch](/?date=2026-06-27&category=social#item-3fe341b0a3bf) from **OpenAI** dominated today, but the bigger story was an unprecedented **[US government-mandated limited preview](/?date=2026-06-27&category=social#item-9f3d22f272ec)** restricting broad access to flagship **Sol**, balanced **Terra**, and efficient **Luna**.

- **Sam Altman** fused product news with the restriction, [backing a required red-teaming preview period](/?date=2026-06-27&category=social#item-7ed9aead238c) while objecting to the government choosing customers; **MIT Technology Review** confirmed the **Trump administration** [asked OpenAI to limit the release](/?date=2026-06-27&category=social#item-cf2acbd80802).
- The move sparked sharp policy debate. **Nathan Lambert** [critiqued 'sloppy thinking' on banning open models](/?date=2026-06-27&category=social#item-9d9334fd9bc7), while others like **Matt Shumer** [warned restrictions would worsen access inequality](/?date=2026-06-27&category=social#item-891f67017631) and concentrate AGI-era power.
- On capabilities, OpenAI touted **Sol** as its [most capable cybersecurity model](/?date=2026-06-27&category=social#item-78707780f9d6) and a [Terminal-Bench 2.1 leader](/?date=2026-06-27&category=social#item-2cecc3815699); **Epoch AI** showcased **Claude Opus 4.7** [autonomously building software in 14 hours](/?date=2026-06-27&category=social#item-1b6772889fbf) for $251.
- **François Chollet** added evergreen nuance, arguing [static-dataset benchmarks measure memorization](/?date=2026-06-27&category=social#item-6fdb4a372c8a) and retrieval, not intelligence, amid the launch hype.

items.title: Good new first: Sol is a smart, efficient, and a significant step forward. It is the same price as G...
items.content:
Good new first: Sol is a smart, efficient, and a significant step forward. It is the same price as GPT-5.5. Also launching in the GPT-5.6 family is Terra, with 5.5-level performance at half the price.

Bad news: at the request of the US government, it is launching today in limited preview instead of the open access launch we were planning on. We are working with the government to get to general availability as fast as we can.

I think it is quite reasonable to roll out models--especially as they reach significant new levels of capability--in this way. It fits with our long-held strategy of iterative deployment. But this isn't quite the process that we think is optimal.

Now we will with the government to attempt to get to a transparent, reliable process for early access, and to ensure that as long as our safeguards work as intended we can release widely. We want to be a reliable, dependable partner that works with all stakeholders, and we also want to live by our mission of benefiting all of humanity. I believe the government shares most of our goals, and that they are overall doing a good job in a very difficult situation.

We will work as quickly as we can to get this model in your hands and we hope you will love it.

items.title: If your benchmark relies on a static dataset or sampling from a static distribution densely known at...

items.content:
Excited to see @cohere open-source how they use AI coding agents to maintain their vLLM fork. 🙌

Keeping a long-lived fork in sync is tricky. Their agent treats it as a control loop: rebase onto each upstream release, run tests, diagnose, fix, repeat until green. Weeks of work down to days.

Bonus: the skills are open source (cohere-ai/vllm-skills) and the agent's fix went back upstream for everyone. 🔧

Worth a read 👇

It is a very difficult culture at Meta to navigate at the moment, I have heard from many a current and former employee.

Plus Apple has Formula 1. Which means all the rich people aren’t moving ecosystems easily. And why every Apple exec I have talked to is not worried. Knows it can come in very late and still take the market.

OpenAI has a shot at disrupting Apple. Meta? Not so much.

Audio headsets are only a temporary game. But they could last for years.

The real game is AI that can see and show. Requires glasses with color displays in both eyes.

The video I just did with a Chinese AI glasses startup is very revealing as to marketing as well as the digital eyewear market.

items.title:
Oh @om…

When I first landed in Silicon Valley, Om Malik helped shape the journalist I became. He ta...
items.content:
Oh @om…

When I first landed in Silicon Valley, Om Malik helped shape the journalist I became. He taught me not just to cover companies, but to understand the people behind them, question the hype, and never lose sight of the bigger picture.

He was a frequent guest on our show, even co-hosting with me at times, and he always brought the spice. You never had to wonder what Om really thought. He was fearless, deeply thoughtful, and never afraid to tell the tech industry hard truths, whether people wanted to hear them or not.

I adored him so much that I brought him home to meet my kids. They knew him as "Uncle Om." And the joy he took in seeing them led me to discover his softer side.

As this AI moment continues to unfold I’ll often wonder what he might have to say about it. One thing I know for certain: he'd have told us exactly what he thought.

We'll miss you, Uncle Om. Thank you for everything.

items.title: I get feedback a lot that is like "your book should be the RL for LLMs book" or "the post-training b...
items.content:
I get feedback a lot that is like "your book should be the RL for LLMs book" or "the post-training book" and it's definitely true those would sell more copies. 

The reality is that this book was in many ways a side project, and by the time I realized I agreed with a bit of this I didn't have the time for *another* refactor.

At the end of the day, I still dumped as much knowledge as I could from what I was doing into the book, and now the course and the code. In it's spirit the book is totally a post-training book. 

The process to change this would've delayed the book from anywhere from 3 to 15 months. It is simply an amount of time I didn't have with Interconnects, Olmo, and other life necessities.

So this isn't to say that I'll never do it. Re-prints and new versions are a common thing. It's doable for me to refactor most of the chapters, re-write the introduction, and make it a post-training centric book.

Still, RLHF as a topic deserves a dedicated text and is far from solved. It's a technology that skyrocketed language models to prominence and points to a lot of fundamental problems interfacing the user and the AI. 

Much of the content that got me to where I am today in my career is by diving into caring about this interface, so I'm happy for it to have the space to live, breath and thrive.

So in reality, I probably could've hot-swapped the title to sell more copies, but it would have made me feel dishonest to do so. For anyone wanting to learn post-training, there's nothing in this book that doesn't apply to you -- post-training is just constantly evolving and growing in complexity.

A final nitpick, is that RLHF actually matches my more conceptual, intuitive vibe a good amount. Post-training is far more practical, in a data and systems sense, where this is more of a math  & intuition book.

Anyways, the RLHF "post-training" Book is coming soon and thank you for trusting me with your attention. 🩵

items.title:
New paradigm for deep research models!

Apodex-1.0-H is a new model that introduces a completely new...
items.content:
New paradigm for deep research models!

Apodex-1.0-H is a new model that introduces a completely new way of working. Apodex comes in several flavors, including an open-weight Apodex-1.0-mini and 0.8B, 2B, and 4B Smol series of models.

This is what's new with Apodex:

1. The model works like a team
2. It dynamically improves its own work
3. It verifies every answer

First, the model works natively like a team of subagents to solve a task:

You have the main agent decomposing the query and spawning specialist sub-agents on demand. All of these subagents work asynchronously. You have agents for research, verification, fact-checking, and auditing, all working together.

I call this "natively" because the ability to decompose a task into multiple subagents was trained into the model.

Second, the model can dynamically improve its own answers.

As it works to produce an answer, the model grades it, notes where it's weak, and rewrites it based on that feedback.

loop: generate → verify → revise

Each pass of this loop learns from the last and improves on the answer.

The grader never sees a correct answer. It's judging whether its own work holds up, with nothing to compare it to.

Third, the model uses different agents to reason and verify.

The obvious hallucinations are easy to catch, but those where the answers look and feel right are much harder and more dangerous.

Models that check their own work have huge blind spots.

The verification process in Apodex uses a separate team of subagents to score answers on several categories to determine which answer actually solves the problem.

You can find the open-weight variants of Apodex on HuggingFace: https://t.co/JMLKlnq3av

To see Apodex-1.0-H in action, go here: https://t.co/YSnQtSq88T

Thanks to the Apodex team for partnering with me on this post.

items.content:
This is the new bear case for SpaceX. 

Well, this is another investor who doesn't understand how to invest in innovation.

SpaceX is an extraordinarily expensive stock if you look backward. But I talked to a room of billionaires the morning that Mark Zuckerberg bought Instagram for a billion dollars, and that didn't have a dollar in sales that day.

Investors in SpaceX tell me they are investing in a future where there are tens of thousands of data centers over your head.

I am visiting one of those kinds of companies in July, in or near Seattle.

 I've learned many times that Elon loves taking on entire industries of naysayers, like this guy.

And he usually doesn't miss.
 https://t.co/7dNB0TmVuX

Locomotion is largely solved.

Loop engineering is a framework for turni...
items.content:
/2 Prompting agents by hand is starting to look outdated.

Loop engineering is a framework for turning coding agents into repeatable workflows. Instead of prompting Grok, Claude Code, Codex, or Cursor one step at a time, you define a recursive goal and let the agent iterate with memory, state, verification, sub-agents, and human handoff.

It also includes CLIs for scaffolding loops, estimating token cost, auditing repos, adding verification gates, and running agents through GitHub Actions. 

Repo: https://t.co/9yonAzcrif

items.content:
Everyone's fighting over who has the best AI agent.

Almost no one's asking what happens when that agent tries to pay for something or when compliance and reporting need to update in real time.

Agents can decide in seconds. But money still moves at human speed with manual auth, 48hr settlement, spreadsheets.

Intelligence without infrastructure stalls.

@airwallex is betting on the fix: T:0 (AI-native finance platform bookkeeping, continuous) + Airi (a wallet built for agentic commerce).

The interface layer gets the headlines. Infrastructure decides if any of it works.

It is an LLM-powered framework...
items.content:
/4 HyperExtract turns messy documents into actual knowledge systems.

It is an LLM-powered framework for converting unstructured text into strongly typed Knowledge Abstracts. It can extract simple lists, Pydantic models, knowledge graphs, hypergraphs, and spatio-temporal graphs.

Use it for research papers, earnings reports, private knowledge bases, or MCP-powered querying from Claude Desktop and IDE agents. 

It also ships with 80+ YAML templates across finance, legal, medical, industry, and general domains. 

Repo: https://t.co/qdf2Ap0mSq

items.content:
/1 Your agent doesn’t need more prompts. It needs better ways to think.

The creative-ideation skill gives Hermes a library of 22 ideation methods inspired by artists, thinkers, and creative frameworks. 

It reads the user’s situation, chooses the method that fits, then generates ideas that are specific, useful, and less obvious than a normal brainstorm.

Useful for product ideas, writing angles, research questions, art projects, gift concepts, or any open-ended prompt where your agent keeps playing it safe. 

Repo: https://t.co/obSKunwIBK

items.summary_html: Scoble recounts Douglas Engelbart's mother of all demos and predictions about future computing including computing gloves.
items.content_html:
@SHELLEYBLEND He is one of the most important figures in modern technology and did the "mother of all demos" that basically laid out the Macintosh 20 years before the Macintosh.

Before he died, he showed me other things that are still to come, including gloves that will let you compute in a new way.

It's quite an interesting dude to study, and I'm glad to further his memory, lest he be forgotten too early.

items.title:

It was an absolute honor to share the stage wit...
items.content:
That's a wrap for Google I/O Connect Berlin 2026 :)

It was an absolute honor to share the stage with such an incredible crew: @SeraAndroid, @rickyrobinett, @anomisSi, @FMuntenescu & @Paul_Kinlan! And live-demoing during the keynote together was a lot of fun(and only slightly nerve-wracking😅)!

Beyond the talks, the best part of these events is always the energy of the community. Connecting with builders, GDG members, GDEs, and customers reminds me why we do what we do.

Thank you to everyone who stopped by to chat or watch our sessions, and a huge thank you to everyone behind the scenes who helped make this happen🙏 !

Until next time!🚀

items.title:
Eight years ago, my great friend @darrenjsmith got oneshotted by Ayahuasca

He was never the same, t...
items.content:
Eight years ago, my great friend @darrenjsmith got oneshotted by Ayahuasca

He was never the same, trying "LSD, Weed, MDMA, DMT, Peyote, San Pedro, Ketamine, Mushrooms" and to me he became something of a psychonaut "looking through the lens of many portals of perspective in consciousness" as he described it

Not completely in a bad way though, he always had very interesting perspectives on things, thinking very out of the box, although I couldn't completely follow him early as he was way way way too far out for me

But he's been slowly returning to Earth as I call it and is shipping again, he's set a $100K MRR goal by end of 2026

In a way he's exactly what I mean when I say "become a more original person" so you can get more unique ideas and a more unique execution of building a product or business, he's been in and out of all the portals of consciousness (way more than me, but I did a bit too :D) and is generally just a very interesting "original" person, so it's likely he has an advantage in terms of making products

Especially now that the technical part is done by AI!

A great friend and a cool follow @darrenjsmith

items.title: A good chunk of Tokyo is also reclaimed from the bay, a process that continues to this day but has s...
items.content:
A good chunk of Tokyo is also reclaimed from the bay, a process that continues to this day but has slowed down significantly (peak was in the 1960s-1970s alongside other major infrastructure buildups).

It's not due to regulation: land reclamation has diminishing returns and conflicts with existing coastline usage. Easier to do when you're also building the rest of the city at the same time.

items.title: Meanwhile, in Quebec City, Canada, I’m sure this year will mark the start of a new kind of global mi...
items.content:
Meanwhile, in Quebec City, Canada, I’m sure this year will mark the start of a new kind of global migration: wealthy and middle-class people will move from their developed but too-hot countries to countries with better climates.

It turns out that the best places to be in the long term are the northern states of the US, Canada, and the Nordic countries of Europe. But Nordic countries are already too expensive and will become even more expensive due to the migration.
